speaker: interviewer
text: It is December the 29th, 1996. The survivor is Reverend Ernest Levy. The interviewer is Herman Hirschberger. The location is London, England, and the language is English. My name is Herman Hirschberger. I'm interviewing Reverend Ernest Levy. The location is London, England, and the language is English. Could you please give me your full name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ernest Levy, although I have a middle name as well-- Ernest Moshe Levy. I was born in Bratislava in 1925. I'm the eight child of a Jewish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you spell your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Levy-- L-E-V-Y. Ernest-- E-R-N-E-S-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames apart from your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin, because I was the youngest in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date of birth as I said, the 25-- sorry, the 13th of January, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age now is 71.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your father and your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Leopold Lowy. In Bratislava, he was called Leopold Lowy. Mother's name was Terezia Hauser.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Lowy?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-W-Y, with an umlaut on the O.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an agent. He was in business. He had marvelous agencies, about five major agencies, in Slovakia-- knitwear what his main line.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he live in Bratislava all his life up to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Father had Hungarian background-- he was born in Hungary, right? And served in the Hungarian-Austrian army in World War I. And then he settled in Vienna. And eventually from Vienna, he moved to Bratislava. There he met my late mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he speak about his experience as a soldier in World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot, an awful lot, he spoke about his experience [INAUDIBLE], yes. And a Jewish soldier in-- in an army, you know-- yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult to keep his Orthodox Judaism in the army, yes, he told us about that. But he managed, more or less, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know whether he was actually fighting at the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not in action. He was not in action. He told us he was just a supply-- in the supply troops, who supplied the army. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, what is her background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's background is a wonderful background. Her-- her-- my grandmother from my mother side comes from Holland-- [NON-ENGLISH] a very wonderful family-- who eventually settled, also, in Bratislava-- which was then Pressburg. And she also comes from a fairly large family-- very, very good background on my mother's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us when Pressburg became Bratislava, and the reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pressburg became Bratislava when Czechoslovakia became a country after World War I. Then Bratislava became the capital of Slovakia. And so there was-- but for many people, it was always Pressburg, which the Slovaks didn't like, certainly. They wanted it to be called Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language you spoke from birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were brought up, actually, in three languages. German was the language spoken at home. We attended German schooling-- schools. The second language was Hungarian. And then Slovakian, we had to learn Slovakish in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although this school was German speaking, was it a public school, or was it denominational?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a public Jewish school, a Jewish public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ethos of the school orthodox or secular?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say orthodox. I would say orthodox. No, I mean, the primary school was more-- more secular, and as it advanced into-- into secondary school, so that became more and more religious. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But the state paid for the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The state paid, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you as well as go to a school go to a Jewish religious class?

speaker: interviewee
text: As we started primary-- as we started secondary school, certainly we attended the [NON-ENGLISH], in the Yeshiva K'Tana. And that what they create emphasis on Jewish education. For the primary important, the [NON-ENGLISH], and the preparation for the Yeshiva. And so, they-- the secondary school actually only started at 4 o'clock from 4:00 to 7:00 in the evening. It was very hard, very long day in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you say something about the family life at home, which was devout?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family life, what a happy family. It was a very happy family with singing, and praying, and eating, and singing, and music from morning till night. There were eight children, and there were seven very talented children with good voices. And father and mother had good voices. They are both come from an artistic background. But on mother's side there were artists, and actors, and preachers, and singers. And on father's side, cantors, Hazanim-- fourth, fifth generation back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Music played a considerable part. Was it music academically studied, or merely synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, it was mainly synagogue. But as I said, we were so in love with music, with all kinds of music, too much in love music. Instead of studying it, we just made music, basking along-- all kinds of opera and auditoriums and everything, without even studying properly. That was a tragedy. And then the war broke out, and we were not prepared for life.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the prewar years, and during that strong musical era, did your family put on performances or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for instance for Purim, for Hanukkah, and for occasions like these, we did put out performances. Our family, our brothers and sisters, they-- they made up an absolute perfect choir. Every color of voice was present-- represented in our family choir-- yes, bass, baritone, tenor, mesosoprano, soprano, alto. I mean, I had a wonderful voice, and I was kind of a child prodigy. And I had to sing from morning to night, and-- and perform and sing and perform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a synagogue choir as such?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I sang in the synagogue choir from the age of five. Yes, from the age of five, I was involved in synagogue singing, in choir.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father have an official position in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, yeah, he started off as a professional cantor in Italy. And then in Vienna, he gave up and he turned toward business. And he-- all his life he stayed an honorary cantor who loved Hazzanut and the liturgical music, but as an honorary-- on an honorary basis, not in professional capacity.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father take an interest in other classical music?

speaker: interviewee
text: He loved-- he loved the Lieder-- the German Lieder. Which, that love for Germany Lieder, he got from my late mother, because she was full of it-- with Schubert, and Schumann, and Brahms Lieder, and [NON-ENGLISH] Lieder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- can you perhaps say, with all this music background, what are your earliest childhood memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: The earliest childhood memories are wonderful-- wonderful. I mean, I was spoiled, certainly. Being the youngest, my sisters just simply adored me. And with my talent, with my singing, I conquered really truly-- I was sometimes mocked and I had to perform, and perform, and singing, and music. And that was quite a happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period, what was the main language at home? German?

speaker: interviewee
text: German, yes, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: During those years, was there any political awareness in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. We had to mature very, very early. In that part of the world, you matured very, very early because you lived in a hostile environment. On the surface, everything seemed to be OK. Life seemed to be going on on a normal basis. But underneath it was boiling, and we felt it. It was an hostile environment. It was Germans-- a lot of Germans-- who disliked the Slovaks. The Slovaks disliked enormously the Hungarians. And they all disliked the Jews. And instead of being a colorful, lovely city, there was a lot of animosity. Coming home from school without being abused, or even beaten up, was an exception. So mother, or father, or brother had to come to meet us halfway so that we could get home safely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived-- but eventually, it became a normal way of life. You see, you get used to this condition, and you live quite happily, in spite of that hostile environment-- which is definitely felt by everybody. Are we speaking here about the late '20s or the early '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early '30s. Early '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the influence of Nazi Germany something that was known to you as a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. We had friends who suddenly appeared and said, I can't come to you anymore. I joined the Nazi Youth Movement. And you looked at him and he was dressed up in the Nazi Youth Movement uniform and said, I'm not coming. I'm not allowed to play with you anymore, you're Jewish people. I-- and I joined the Nazi movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were aware what's going on. And that was the tragedy that we were aware, and we still didn't take it seriously. We didn't want to recognize what's going on, that's boiling underneath. So it was rather similar to life in Nazi Germany in this respect.

speaker: interviewee
text: Similar, similar, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Hitler Youth, were they directed from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Directed from Germany. Directed from Germany. So the German Nazi influence was increasing by the day in that part of the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the government of Czechoslovakia was a democratic--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was the-- that was supposed to be the democracy par excellence, Czechoslovakia with Tomas Garrigue Masaryk. Right? And he meant it well. And it worked for a while, until really the Nazi regime came to power, and the Nazi influence started spreading.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the Hitler Youth, although you went to a Jewish school, what how was your contact with the non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not good. Not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the contact arise?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, for instance, we liked football, and we'd play football against each other. And it always ended up in a-- in-- in a terrible fight, and to be beaten up, and so on. It was not good. It was not good. The relationship-- they were the-- there was hostility underneath everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that tender age, what thoughts did you have about this? What emotions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I witnessed my family getting serious warnings about sitting on a time bomb. So my uncle Charles, who went to Palestine-- then called Palestine, now it's Israel-- in 1936, he pleaded with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can't you see how [NON-ENGLISH]? Can't you see what's happening to the world? Give me some of your children. Don't keep everybody here in that-- in that uncertainty. Can't you see what's coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1937, he came back to Bratislava. Again, he pleaded with my father-- please, move, go! [NON-ENGLISH]. Move! Go! Father didn't take it seriously. Right, and so that was the great tragedy. We didn't want to recognize the danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people-- many people did. And that was [NON-ENGLISH], what we say in the [NON-ENGLISH]-- who can see the consequences of their action, or their lack of action? These were the people. And my Charles-- my uncle Charles died in Bat Yam, near Tel Aviv, at the age of 93. And my father, my wonderful little father, perished in Buchenwald at the age of 59. So if you have got the picture, you can't blame God. Right? You can't blame him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He sent-- he sent Charles. He sent the warnings. We were actually refusing to recognize the danger. And we paid the price.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how your parents thought about these exhortations to emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just could not make up there minds to move. Now. Uprooting is easier said than done, as you know, with a large family-- a beautiful home, [NON-ENGLISH], a beautiful living in business-- it's easier said than done. You must have the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the faith in God. That's what we call faith in God-- that you can pull yourself up and say, I'm moving, I'm going. Right? That's a real [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What percentage of people accepted the warning and left?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10%- 15%, yes, did go, move, yes. And mother felt very strongly about it. Maybe we should do something. I remember father coming home from school in Bratislava, still in July, 1938, was attacked on the street. And he came home in a terrible state-- his arm, hanging, broken. And then he said to mother, and to the family, [NON-ENGLISH]-- the alarm, it's ringing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then he said, but today's Shabbos-- Shabbat. Don't let it spoil our Shabbat. Let's have our dinner, blah, blah, and [NON-ENGLISH], you know. Just another dance, another Shabbat. And we had another Shabbat, and everything was forgotten-- typical, right? And nothing. The alarm-- the alarm rang-- so what? Another Shabbat, another yontif, and [NON-ENGLISH], right? That's what we call, we were the victims of hope. This is so typical, right? Hope-- what was the hope? Ah, the war will end soon, number one. And [NON-ENGLISH] is just around the corner, right? What else? It can't happen to us, right? And that was really how whole communities, they're paralyzed by hope-- by that hope-and-pray syndrome instead of pulling yourself up, and act, and go, and move. So what was the Jewish population of Bratislava approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around-- approximately 20,000, of which 800 survived-- between 800 and 900 survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the community was mainly orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly orthodox, a very orthodox community. Yes, it was-- the culture in Bratislava was overshadowed by Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your thought when your father came home with a broken arm on Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I was 13. Right? 13. When he said, the alarm is going, let's go. I said, yes, yes father, let's go. Because my third brother Fritz said to the father, I think that is now the moment-- let's go! And they were standing there and-- let's go. Let's go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What action did subsequently was taken by the family after Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No action was taken whatsoever. Remember that was July. On November the 4th, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] father came home from shul with me and my brother, Alexander, right-- and we didn't make Kiddush. But usually we come from shul, we make [HEBREW] you make Kiddush, you have your meal. Hours went by, no Kiddush. Father was sitting because there were rumors in shul already that Jewish families are being thrown out of their homes, especially with Hungarian background. And we had that Hungarian background. And, eh, terrible. And then I say it-- father, Kiddush. It's half past 8:00. Let's [INAUDIBLE] November.

speaker: interviewee
text: 00. All right, my kin, let's make Kiddush and-- he just wanted to start [NON-ENGLISH], and the knock came on the door. And there was a German and a Czechoslovakian police officer, and gave us 10 minutes to get out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was--

speaker: interviewee
text: November the 4th, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some weeks or months after Germany invaded Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Invaded Sudetenland. Slovakia had not invaded, but there was already the Slovak fascists took over. the Hlinka Guard, took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was still Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still Czechoslovakia, but just no more, no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got 10 minutes to get out of our beautiful house. All the lights were on. And we knew, we heard that the lights were on for months afterwards. The house was sealed up. The beautiful Friday night dinner-- Shabbos dinner-- all there, ready. And we were put into buses-- hundreds of Jewish families, children, babies, elderly, sick people-- and taken to a no-man's land, a two-hour drive towards the Hungarian border. It was-- the weather was atrocious, terrible-- sleet, and snow, and ice, and cold. And then we were thrown out of the buses-- hundreds and hundreds of families, babies, elderly. We didn't know where we were. In the mud-- ankle-deep mud. On the 4th of November. And that was already late night, Friday night. One moment you are-- you've got a home, you've got a family, you've got a life, you've got a name, you've got everything. So that was Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one little single light in a very distant [INAUDIBLE]. There was not even a tree to take shelter. Imagine that. We were soaked and cold. So we went towards that little light, and that was Hungary. So we dragged ourselves into Hungary. And the community there, in Hungary was a little town called Dunajska Streda-- Dunajska Streda. And they were marvelous. I can only say they were marvelous. By the time we dragged ourselves into Dunajska Streda. The people were just going to shul. It was already shul time, Shabbos morning. There's a big [INAUDIBLE], right? Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Imagine how we looked-- soaked, full of mud, crying children, babies, women. Imagine, that was 10 kilometers. We had to walk 10 kilometers into Dunajska Streda.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the [NON-ENGLISH] went up, gave a big clop of the beam, and he says, look what happened to Bratislava Jewry. We will have to help. And they were marvelous. They gave the-- the rabbi gave permission, certainly, to take the carts and the cars to bring the people from the mud into-- on Shabbat-- onto Dunajska Streda. So that was the start of our Holocaust. That was the start. And from Dunajska Streda, the family eventually-- Before we go further--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me, this action of being thrown out of your home, was that orchestrated by Germans or by Czechs?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was by the Slovak fascists, it was orchestrated, under the influence of the German neighborhood. That was-- it was revenge, because you must remember, at the time, Slovakia had to hand over to Hungary, the-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know in English-- the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the-- you know-- the overland. Like-- like-- territories they had to hand back. That was promised by the Fuhrer, Horty got the promise, you will get these territories-- and the Slovaks were bitter about it, very bitter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the first revenge. And who suffers first? The Jewish population. So it was a promise to seed some of the uplands of Slovakia to Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Highlands

speaker: interviewer
text: The Highlands.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were so bitter about it, the Slovaks, and we had to suffer first. We were the first that they call today ethnic cleansing, but in a very, very harsh way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did what you say apply to the whole Jewish population of Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet. That was only the Jewish population if of Hungarian background. And there were many, many, but it didn't apply yet to the whole population of Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was several hundreds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, several hundreds, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue what happened once you got into Hungary and the community there received you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The community there was absolutely fantastic. On Shabbat, everybody had where to sleep on that night. I remember even, on Shabbat's morning, we had already a wonderful Kiddush there. The doors were open to us. And they did whatever humanly possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But how long can you stay? We stayed one week there. I stayed with a family, Vice, who were just wonderful-- too wonderful for words how they treated us. Because we were already refugees, remember-- refugees! And eventually the family-- I went to Papa, to grandfather [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] alone. Papa being the city in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary. The grandfather was a cantor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that? Did your family stay together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not for a while, only later on in 1939, well into 1939. In the meantime, the war broke out and the family met in Budapest, including my brother Charles, who fled Munich the morning after the Kristallnacht. And we all met-- he didn't know about us being thrown out of Bratislava. We didn't know about him thrown out-- running away from Munich. But we met in a big shul in Budapest. We all met.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- being thrown out of Czechoslovakia happened about the same time as the Polish Jews were thrown out of Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, about the same time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did any news of Kristallnacht get through at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did get through, yes. It did get through, yes. It did get through. And remember that the Kristallnacht triggered off an awful lot of problems for our people. After the Kristallnacht, things started going down, down, down, down-- never stopped going down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although you were only a little boy, was it known to you what happened to Jews in Germany--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: On Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. We knew, we knew, we knew, yes, what happened in Germany. Yes. And remember, that was also after the anschluss-- we knew exactly what's going on in Vienna-- the degradation and humiliation of the creme de la creme of Vienna Austrian Jews. And still, we didn't take it too seriously. We lived so near to Vienna, and still we did not think it will-- you know-- you hope, you hope, and that's your downfall.
